query: How do Buddhism and Hinduism differ on the concept of self?
response: Buddhism and Hinduism offer distinct perspectives on the concept of self. In Buddhism, the idea of "anatta" or ["non-self"](https://siftersearch.com/library/Buddhist/Pali%20Canon/tr-nanamoli-thera_majjhima-nikaya-9-sammaditthi-sutta-the-discourse-on-right-view#p16) suggests that there is no permanent, unchanging self. This view is central to understanding the nature of suffering and liberation in Buddhist teachings. In contrast, Hinduism often embraces the concept of "atman," which refers to the eternal self or soul that is part of the universal Brahman. This belief in an enduring self is foundational to many Hindu philosophies and practices. So, while Buddhism denies a permanent self, Hinduism affirms it as a core element of its spiritual framework.
